articles.title: Follow Protopica by RSS
articles.author: Manuel Sainsily & Will Selviz
articles.content:
Our Research page now has an RSS feed.

We added a small RSS subscribe button so people can follow Protopica updates without needing to check the website manually.

Whenever we publish a new article, field note, experiment, or process update, the feed will update automatically. It is a simple way to keep a slower, more intentional connection to our work without depending only on social platforms or algorithms.

How to subscribe on desktop

1. Install an RSS reader extension such as Feeder for Chrome.

2. Open the Protopica Research page and click Subscribe via RSS, or paste this feed URL into your reader:

https://protopica.com/blog/rss.xml

3. New Protopica articles will appear directly in your reader when they are published.

How to subscribe on iPhone or iPad

1. Download an RSS app such as the Feeder.co iOS app.

2. Add a new feed and paste:

https://protopica.com/blog/rss.xml

3. You can now receive Protopica updates from your phone, without waiting for a social media post to surface.

RSS is old internet infrastructure, but it still feels right for what we are building: a quieter way to gather around the work, follow the signal, and return when something new is ready.

articles.title: Introducing Protopica 2.0
articles.author: Manuel Sainsily & Will Selviz
articles.content:
Today, we are launching Protopica 2.0:


A clearer home for the work we do and the people we hope to build with.

Protopica is a design research lab that turns emerging technologies into meaningful public experiences. We consult, build, and teach across immersive exhibitions, worldbuilding, cultural preservation, creative technology, and education.

Our new website brings that work together in one place: projects tested with real audiences, collaborations with creators and organizations, lectures and workshops, and the team behind the practice.

We also wanted the website itself to reflect how we think. It begins around a digital fire, then opens into a slower, more intentional space where analog storytelling and computational aesthetics meet.

This release is a foundation for what comes next. We will use it to share new work, field notes, open experiments, and practical ways to build something new without losing what makes it human.

Welcome to Protopica 2.0.
